perturbation.original_text: return any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever in his or her possession, or to delete forever any ConfidentialInformation that he or she may have stored on a computer or other device, atthe request of the other party, except that each party may keep one copy in its confidential legal files for the purpose of assuring compliance with this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: return any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever in his or her possession, or to delete any Confidential Information that he or she may have stored on a computer or other device.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit allowance for each party to keep one copy of the confidential information in its legal files for compliance purposes. This negatively impacts Augusta University since it removes their right to keep a copy for compliance purposes and increases the risk that the other party doesn't maintain documentation for compliance. This introduces legal risk because such a clause is important for record-keeping in the event of legal disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 10-1-761 relates to trade secrets and maintaining their confidentiality. While not directly mandating retention of records, the removal of the clause allowing retention for compliance undermines the ability to demonstrate reasonable efforts to protect the trade secret, potentially weakening a future claim if the information is leaked.

Definitions Universal Citation: GA Code § 10-1-761 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next As used in this article, the term: “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to maintain secrecy or limit use, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering of a trade secret not acquired by misappropriation or independent development shall not be considered improper means. “Misappropriation” means: Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: Used improper means to acquire knowledge of a trade secret; At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that knowledge of the trade secret was: Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint v
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly allowed each party to retain a copy of confidential information for compliance purposes. The changed text removes this allowance. The Law Explanation argues that removing the clause allowing retention for compliance undermines the ability to demonstrate reasonable efforts to protect the trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Georgia Code § 10-1-761, defines 'Misappropriation' and 'Improper means', highlighting the importance of maintaining secrecy. By removing the allowance to keep a copy for compliance, the modified contract weakens the ability of Augusta University to protect their trade secrets, creating a legal risk and thus a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS , unauthorized disclosure of any confidential information to any person or entity will cause irreparable harm to the parties;
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS , unauthorized disclosure of any confidential information to any person or entity may cause harm to the parties;
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the acknowledgment of harm from "irreparable harm" to "may cause harm". Georgia law regarding injunctions and equitable relief often relies on a showing of irreparable harm. Removing this explicit acknowledgment potentially makes it more difficult for Augusta University to obtain an injunction against disclosure, which could violate intellectual property law.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 9-5-1 discusses the power of courts to grant relief. Removing the acknowledgement of 'irreparable harm' weakens the legal basis for Augusta University to seek injunctive relief (a common remedy in NDA breaches) under Georgia law, as showing irreparable harm is a crucial factor in obtaining an injunction.

Next Equity, by a writ of injunction, may restrain proceedings in another or the same court, a threatened or existing tort, or any other act of a private individual or corporation which is illegal or contrary to equity and good conscience and for which no adequate remedy is provided at law. History. Orig. Code 1863, § 3137; Code 1868, § 3149; Code 1873, § 3210; Code 1882, § 3210; Civil Code 1895, § 4913; Civil Code 1910, § 5490; Code 1933, § 55-101. Law reviews. For note advocating reassessment of state authority towards injunctions in labor disputes, see 18 Mercer L. Rev. 461 (1967). For article discussing validity of ex parte injunction affecting constitutionally protected rights, see 7 Ga. L. Rev. 246 (1973). For article, “State Court Injunctions in Labor Disputes,” see 10 Ga. St. B.J. 559 (1974). For note, “The Ongoing Royalty: What Remedy Should a Patent Holder Receive When a Permanent Injunction Is Denied,” see 43 Ga. L. Rev. 543 (2009). Next Disclaimer: These
perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledged "irreparable harm," which is a significant factor in obtaining injunctive relief. The changed text weakens this to "may cause harm," potentially making it more difficult for Augusta University to secure an injunction. The Law Explanation highlights that showing irreparable harm is crucial for injunctive relief. Scraped Law Snippet 2 (Georgia Code § 9-5-1) discusses the purposes for which injunctions may be issued. While it doesn't explicitly mention "irreparable harm," it indicates that injunctions are for acts "for which no adequate remedy is provided at law." The change weakens the original assertion, making it harder to argue that there's no adequate remedy at law, and therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: the parties may disclose Confidential Information to their directors, officers, employees, advisors and representatives, provided that these persons are bound by the duty of non-disclosure in this agreement and are informed of this duty.
perturbation.changed_text: the parties may disclose Confidential Information to their directors, officers, employees, advisors and representatives.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement that disclosed parties (directors, officers, etc.) are bound by the duty of non-disclosure and informed of this duty. This is a significant weakening of the confidentiality protection, especially because these are authorized personnel who could cause widespread damage. This introduces legal risk because it increases the likelihood of unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 10-1-762 addresses misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the requirement that directors, officers, etc., are bound by the NDA, the agreement fails to take reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of the information, potentially weakening a claim for misappropriation under Georgia law if one of those individuals discloses the information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Georgia Code § 10-1-762 (2022) - Injunctive Relief :: 2022 Georgia Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code 2022 Georgia Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Georgia Code Title 10 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 1 - Selling and Other Trade Practices Article 27 - Trade Secrets § 10-1-762. Injunctive Relief Universal Citation: GA Code § 10-1-762 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in appropriate circumstances for reasons including, but not limited to, an elimination of commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation or where the trade secret ceases to exist due to the fault of the enjoined party or others by improper means. In exceptional circumstances, if the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. In no event shall a contract be required in order to maintain an action or to obtain injunctive relief for misappropriation of a trade secret. History. Code 1981, § 10-1-762 , enacted by Ga. L. 1990, p. 1560, § 1. Law reviews. For article, “Application of the ‘In
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the requirement that individuals with access to confidential information (directors, officers, etc.) are bound by a duty of non-disclosure. According to the law explanation, this failure to take reasonable measures to protect confidentiality could weaken a claim for misappropriation under Georgia law, as it relates to O.C.G.A. § 10-1-762. The scraped snippet from Georgia Code § 10-1-762 discusses injunctive relief for actual or threatened misappropriation, implying that protecting against misappropriation is a legal concern. By removing the non-disclosure obligation for authorized personnel, the contract increases the risk of misappropriation.
